note: Ladder rung 2: GDSF (Cherkasova 1998), the first cost-consulting policy. Under the linear cost model it reduces to LFU-with-aging, so this row measures what the FREQUENCY signal alone collects of LRU's 60-point room over the oracle. Answer: negative, and the canonical version's reset-thrash mechanism was predicted and is confirmed by ablation.
reset_thrash_ablation: Canonical GDSF resets frequency per-residency; the retained-history variant keeps it across residency gaps. On this workload the canonical reset costs ~23 points (203.2 -> 180.5). Mechanism, as predicted: agentic re-reads bring an evicted hot block straight back as a freq-1 stranger, making it the next cheapest victim (a self-reinforcing thrash loop for exactly the hot blocks). Retained history lets the block re-enter with standing, breaking the loop. The prediction was recorded in the GDSF docstring before the run; the ablation confirms it.
finding: Even retained-history GDSF (180.5%) loses to LRU (160.5%): the frequency signal, with or without history, is worse than pure recency on agentic re-reads. Cumulative ladder result so far: no simple single-signal heuristic beats LRU. Recency 160.5% < idle-time (IdleTTL) 176.4% < frequency-with-history 180.5% < frequency-reset 203.2%. The 60 points of room over the oracle are collected by NONE of the classic heuristics; they make it worse. This sharpens the case that lifecycle/workflow information (WA-LRU, retired-cache, gap-aware protection, the economic policy) is what the room requires.
performance: GDSF ran the high-pressure cell in 24.6s, under the ~29s budget; the per-eviction sort survives at 256 granularity, so the heap optimization is again declined (measured, not assumed).
